2PC is more scalable and fault-tolerant, but less consistent and prone to conflicts. Saga is more efficient and consistent, but less scalable and prone to deadlocks.
Saga is more scalable and fault-tolerant, but less consistent and prone to conflicts. 2PC is more efficient and consistent, but less scalable and prone to deadlocks.
questions.answer: 2PC is more efficient and consistent, but less scalable and prone to deadlocks. Saga is more scalable and fault-tolerant, but less consistent and prone to conflicts.
questions.explanation: Evaluating the trade-offs between 2PC and Saga protocols for a distributed database system requires considering performance, consistency, scalability, and fault tolerance. 2PC is generally more efficient and consistent, but less scalable and prone to deadlocks. Saga, on the other hand, is more scalable and fault-tolerant, but less consistent and prone to conflicts.

questions.question: Analyze the trade-offs between clustered and non-clustered indexes for a query pattern involving range queries on a column with varying selectivity. Consider the impact on query performance, storage usage, and maintenance complexity.
options: Clustered indexes are more efficient and scalable, but less flexible and prone to fragmentation. Non-clustered indexes are more flexible and maintainable, but less efficient and prone to storage usage.

Non-clustered indexes are more efficient and scalable, but less flexible and prone to fragmentation. Clustered indexes are more flexible and maintainable, but less efficient and prone to storage usage.

Clustered indexes are more flexible and maintainable, but less efficient and prone to storage usage. Non-clustered indexes are more efficient and scalable, but less flexible and prone to fragmentation.

Non-clustered indexes are more flexible and maintainable, but less efficient and prone to storage usage. Clustered indexes are more efficient and scalable, but less flexible and prone to fragmentation.
questions.answer: Clustered indexes are more efficient and scalable, but less flexible and prone to fragmentation. Non-clustered indexes are more flexible and maintainable, but less efficient and prone to storage usage.
questions.explanation: Evaluating the trade-offs between clustered and non-clustered indexes for a query pattern requires considering query performance, storage usage, and maintenance complexity. Clustered indexes are generally more efficient and scalable, but less flexible and prone to fragmentation. Non-clustered indexes, on the other hand, are more flexible and maintainable, but less efficient and prone to storage usage.

questions.question: Consider a distributed NoSQL system designed for high availability and fault tolerance. Compare the consistency trade-offs between strong, eventual, and causal consistency models for a given workload, considering the impact on throughput, latency, and concurrency level.
options: Strong consistency is more reliable and accurate, but less scalable and prone to performance bottlenecks. Eventual consistency is more scalable and fault-tolerant, but less reliable and prone to data inconsistencies.

Eventual consistency is more reliable and accurate, but less scalable and prone to performance bottlenecks. Causal consistency is more scalable and fault-tolerant, but less reliable and prone to data inconsistencies.

Causal consistency is more reliable and accurate, but less scalable and prone to performance bottlenecks. Strong consistency is more scalable and fault-tolerant, but less reliable and prone to data inconsistencies.

Strong consistency is more scalable and fault-tolerant, but less reliable and prone to data inconsistencies. Eventual consistency is more reliable and accurate, but less scalable and prone to performance bottlenecks.
questions.answer: Strong consistency is more reliable and accurate, but less scalable and prone to performance bottlenecks. Eventual consistency is more scalable and fault-tolerant, but less reliable and prone to data inconsistencies.
questions.explanation: Evaluating the trade-offs between strong, eventual, and causal consistency models for a distributed NoSQL system requires considering consistency, scalability, and fault tolerance. Strong consistency is generally more reliable and accurate, but less scalable and prone to performance bottlenecks. Eventual consistency, on the other hand, is more scalable and fault-tolerant, but less reliable and prone to data inconsistencies.

questions.question: Analyze the trade-offs between MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control) and lock-based concurrency control for a workload involving frequent concurrent updates and high contention. Consider the impact on throughput, latency, and concurrency level.
options: MVCC is more efficient and scalable, but less consistent and prone to versioning conflicts. Lock-based concurrency control is more consistent and fault-tolerant, but less efficient and prone to deadlocks.

Lock-based concurrency control is more efficient and scalable, but less consistent and prone to deadlocks. MVCC is more consistent and fault-tolerant, but less efficient and prone to versioning conflicts.
